data.title: BlackBerry: The Latest Revenue Warning
data.description:
One of the worst performing companies and stocks that I've covered on this site is BlackBerry (NYSE:BB)(TSX:BB:CA). The Canadian technology firm has seen its shares lose more than 90% of their value over the decade plus I've analyzed it. The main culprit has been an inability to grow the company's top line over the long run, consistently missing street expectations. On Wednesday, the company held its Investor Day, and yet again management issued a revenue forecast that was weaker than street estimates.

Previous coverage on the name:

It was a little more than six months ago that I last covered the name, which at the time had started to show some improvement. Fiscal Q4 revenues came in ahead of estimates, and while guidance was weak as usual, a completed debt deal had certainly helped the balance sheet. Given the CEO change that had happened just a few months prior, I was interested to see how the new leadership team would impact things moving forward.

At that time, I wasn't ready to buy into the BlackBerry story just yet. Revenue growth was still an issue, and the financial situation wasn't exactly the greatest. One major saving grace for investors seemed to be a distressed valuation against peers in the space. Unfortunately, since then, BlackBerry shares have lost another 15%, compared to a low double-digit return for the S&P 500, while a number of major tech names are rallying to new highs.

A look back at prior revenue expectations:

Gone are the days when BlackBerry was a major player in the handset business, with total revenues being around $20 billion a year. Shortly after John Chen took the reigns in late 2013, eventually pivoting to a software and services model, he was looking for sustained growth when the company fell under $1 billion in annual revenues. Unfortunately, those plans didn't work out, and once we got through Covid, BlackBerry management unveiled the following aggressive growth plan:

Given the tremendous growth we've seen lately in the cybersecurity industry, low-double digit annual percentage revenue growth isn't considered a high bar for a normal company. Unfortunately, I've covered many guidance cuts from BlackBerry (for example here and here), so my faith in the company's longer-term forecasts has never been that high.

As it turned out, it didn't take that long for those fiscal 2027 hopes to be dashed. It would be just a year later in May 2023 when the company provided a major update to its long-term targets, which basically featured a revenue guidance cut. As the graphic below shows, management still was calling for low double-digit percentage sales growth, but we were now a fiscal year in the future and the previous year's actual was a bit lower.

Another weak revenue forecast:

On Wednesday, BlackBerry management laid out its latest multi-year plan, with a focus on revenues returning to growth and profitability. The good news is that guidance for the current fiscal year, which ends at the end of February 2025, was maintained, with its midpoint being roughly where the street is. However, despite analyst estimates continuing to move lower for fiscal 2026 as seen in the chart below, guidance for $620 million to $650 million was a bit below the street's average estimate at nearly $654 million.

The current guidance does imply some revenue growth for fiscal 2026, but it will be rather limited. In the company's most recent earnings report last month, there were some green shoots, with the cybersecurity business starting to show some improvement in its key metrics for annual recurring revenues and its net retention rate. Management also believes its cost-cutting efforts will get the bottom-line moving in the right direction, and reduced losses should help cut cash burn levels.

One negative here is that the latest company data on QNX shows that its growth is slowing. The company's release states that its software package is now in over 255 million vehicles, which it says is a growth of 20 million in a year. However, the company said it had passed 235 million vehicles already in June 2023. At that time last year, year-over-year growth was 20 million, so it seems like it has taken another 15 plus months to get the next 20 million, and that's off a higher base number (lower percentage growth).

Perhaps the main headwind here in the medium term is the company's balance sheet. While there is no debt due anytime soon, the net cash balance was just $53 million at the end of August. More than half of BlackBerry's assets are comprised of either goodwill or intangible assets as well. Thus, it's not like the company can go out and make any major acquisitions here, even if it uses some amount of stock, to really bolster its revenue base.

A look at the current valuation:

As of Wednesday afternoon, BlackBerry shares were trading for less than 2.5 times their expected revenues for the February 2025 fiscal year. That's significantly below most comparable software companies that go for much more, such as Microsoft (MSFT) at around 10 times or CrowdStrike (CRWD) at nearly 20 times. However, many of these larger names have very strong revenue growth profiles, and some even have sizable GAAP profits and or significant free cash flow generation. I'm still not thrilled with the BlackBerry revenue situation here, but perhaps management was just being conservative with its guidance for a fiscal year that hasn't even started yet.

As for the street, analysts have remained quite positive on this stock, which unfortunately hasn't helped out most investors. The average price target of $3.23 represents more than 20% upside from current levels. However, that average was at nearly $5.50 a year ago and a bit over $8.00 three years ago, so the street has been cutting its valuation on the name over time.

Final thoughts and recommendation:

In the end, BlackBerry's investor day was a bit mixed. While management detailed the progress it is making on certain growth and cost-cutting efforts, revenue guidance for fiscal 2026 still missed dramatically reduced street estimates. There seems to be some top line growth coming, but definitely not anywhere near what was previously planned, so the path back to $1 billion in yearly revenue will take much longer than originally thought.

For the moment, I'm going to keep a hold rating on BlackBerry shares. I'm a bit disappointed in the revenue forecast yet again, but at least the bottom line and cash flow trends appear to be heading in the right direction. With a valuation this low, there is some room for upside if things go right, but we haven't seen that happen in quite a while. I'll need to see a couple of quarters before I can truly buy in, but I could also become negative again if the latest growth plan doesn't pan out.

data.title: Proficient Auto Logistics: Sell On Major Earnings Warning And Weak Near-Term Prospects
data.description:
Proficient Auto Logistics (NASDAQ:PAL) is a combination of five previously independent auto logistics companies ("the founding companies"):

  1. Proficient Auto Transport, Inc.
  2. Delta Automobile Services, Inc.
  3. Deluxe Auto Carriers, Inc.
  4. Sierra Mountain Group, Inc.
  5. Tribeca Automotive Inc.

The company operates one of the largest auto transportation fleets in North America utilizing roughly 1,150 auto transport vehicles and trailers on a daily basis, including approximately 700 Company-owned transport vehicles and trailers.

PAL operates more than 50 facilities across the United States with a primary focus on transporting finished vehicles from automotive production facilities, marine ports of entry or regional rail yards to auto dealerships around the country.

The company has strategic relationships with leading auto original equipment manufacturers ("OEMs") like General Motors (GM), BMW (OTCPK:BAMXF, OTCPK:BMWYY), Stellantis (STLA) and Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGAF, MBGYY) as well electric vehicle producers like Tesla (TSLA) and Rivian (RIVN).

Proficient Auto Logistics raised $212.3 million in net proceeds from its initial public offering in May. The company utilized the majority of these funds for acquiring its founding companies.

In August, PAL acquired Auto Transport Group, LC or "ATG" for $28.9 million in cash and 1.07 million shares of newly issued common stock. The acquisition resulted in net debt increasing from $19 million as of the end of Q2 to approximately $48 million.

While the company's first quarter as a public company showed decent sequential growth, volumes started to weaken in June and further into July as outlined by management in more detail on the Q2/2024 conference call.

(...) we're observing many of the major auto manufacturers have used their second quarter earnings reports to temper second half volume guidance in the face of a weaker than expected volumes last quarter. References to high inventory at dealership lots, declining profitability metrics, and planned cost cutting measures were common theme.

However, the company still projected mid-single-digit sequential revenue growth for the third quarter and low double-digit sequential growth for the seasonally strong fourth quarter, which is still reflected in consensus estimates:

At that time, market participants didn't seem to be overly concerned, but subsequent earnings warnings by several OEMs resulted in the stock losing ground in September and even moving below its $15 IPO price in recent weeks.

With a number of key customers experiencing sales challenges, Wednesday's news can hardly be considered a surprise (emphasis added by author):

Weakness in unit volumes continued throughout the third quarter of 2024, resulting in estimated unit volumes of flat to a decline of 1% compared to combined unit volumes for the third quarter of 2023.

Volume underperformance resulted primarily from significantly less dedicated fleet, brokerage and spot buy opportunities, which are typically at premium pricing, the combined result being an estimated $90 - $92 million in revenue for the third quarter, which represents a decline of 14 - 16% compared to combined revenue for the third quarter of 2023.

Given the reduced level of revenue and resulting loss of operating leverage, the Company expects that net income for the third quarter of 2024 will be significantly degraded compared to results reported for the quarter ended June 30, 2024.

Macroeconomic factors affecting unit volumes include a decline in vehicle SAAR (seasonally adjusted annual sales rates), which for the full quarter ending September 30, 2024, was estimated to be lower by 1.9% compared to the same quarter in 2023.

The Company's management notes industry commentary that vehicle sales were negatively impacted by consumers feeling the adverse effect of an uncertain job market as well as possibly waiting for anticipated interest rate cuts before committing to big ticket purchases such as autos.

At the mid-point of the preliminary range, revenues will be down by approximately 14% on a sequential basis, despite the benefit from the recent acquisition of ATG.

Given current market conditions, it is fair to assume that Q4 revenues will be nowhere close to the $122 million projected by analysts. In addition, expectations for 2025 will likely have to be reduced, too.

With profitability projected to be a far cry from Q2/2024 numbers, I would expect analysts to reduce price targets across the board over the next couple of sessions. Given the magnitude of the earnings miss and persistently adverse market conditions, even downgrades might be in the cards.

With no near-term improvement in sight, I expect the stock to be dead money for at least the next several quarters.

Given this issue, investors should consider moving to the sidelines until North American auto sales start to recover.

Bottom Line

While perhaps not entirely surprising, the sheer magnitude of Wednesday's earnings warning in combination with expectations for weak market conditions to persist in the near term is likely to keep shares in the penalty box for the next several quarters.

Consequently, I am initiating coverage of Proficient Auto Logistics with a "Sell" rating.

data.title: Tesla: Time To Be Careful (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
The market was less than impressed with the Robotaxi presentation of Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) (NEOE:TSLA:CA) a few days ago and, as a matter of fact, it disappointed investors to such an extent that the electric-vehicle company’s stock price decreased 9%.

Tesla’s stock has gotten clobbered in the last year as well as electric-vehicle demand slowed, and the Robotaxi event did not particularly help in changing investors’ mood about Tesla.

While the electric-vehicle company did reveal a driverless Cybercab, expectations widely fell short of expectations, in no small part because Tesla did not make substantial progress on presenting autonomous driving capabilities.

I think that Tesla is a ‘Hold’ after the stock broke through a critical support level, but investors should watch the downside.

Robotaxi Event Is A Dud

Elon Musk’s Tesla revealed a self-driving taxi, a fully autonomous transport vehicle, the Robovan, for up to 20 persons and the company announced that it made some progress in terms of its robot development.

However, the event, which was both delayed and much-hyped, failed to produce any near-term catalysts or showed investors a new income path that could have helped the stock to power higher.

Just days earlier, Tesla released delivery figures for 3Q24, which fell short of consensus expectations. Tesla delivered 462,890 electric-vehicles in the third quarter and though the delivery figure was up more than 6% compared to the year ago period, Tesla missed expectations of 469,828 deliveries.

With that said, though, it was the first quarter of positive growth as both previous two quarters saw declining volumes on a YoY basis.

Tesla Has Probably Seen More Operating Margin Pressure in 3Q24

In the last quarter, growing pressure on deliveries and a cool-down of the market led to pressure on Tesla’s operating margins. In 2Q24, Tesla’s operating margins fell to 6.3%, reflecting a decrease of 333 bps YoY amid heavily discounted car deals and other incentives that weighed on profitability.

In the third quarter, China’s growth came back, but also primarily based on deal availability. This implies that Tesla is probably going to see ongoing margin contraction, which could lead to a lackluster earnings release next week.

The market presently models $0.59 per share in profits for Tesla’s most recent quarter, which compares against $0.66 per share in profits last year. Tesla also missed profit estimates for four straight quarters, with the last quarter of a profit beat dating back to 2Q23.

With no real catalysts in the business and deliveries coming in below expectations, I think Tesla’s stock is likely to remain in a side-ways movement moving forward.

Approaching A Key Level, The 200-Day Moving Average Line

The technical situation of Tesla’s stock can be described critical, as the stock price after the Robotaxi event fell below the 50-day moving average line, which presently runs at $225.31.

Based on the Relative Strength Index, Tesla is trending toward oversold territory, but as of yet the stock is not technically oversold. If Tesla drops toward and below the 200-day moving average line, the technical setup greatly deteriorates. The 200-day moving average trendline currently runs at $202.34 which is also where the stock has robust support as well. If the $202 level breaks, Tesla’s stock could be in real trouble and have much more short-term downside.

The next event that could lead to a retest of the 200-day moving average trend is the earnings release for the third quarter, results of which are expected on October 23, 2024.

Tesla Is Rather Expensive

The market models $3.07 per share in profits for Tesla in 2025, reflecting back to us an anticipated profit jump of 35% YoY. With a stock presently selling at $218, the electric-vehicle company has a profit multiple, on a leading basis, of 71x. To put it mildly, Tesla is not a steal here and investors still seem to have kind of high expectations for Elon Musk’s electric-vehicle company, expectations that might not be justified based on Tesla’s Robotaxi event and delivery growth in 3Q24.

Presently, neither the robot nor autonomous driving businesses appear to offer any products of near-term marketability, and many years may pass before we actually see a market-ready product such as a Cybercab or Robovan.

In the meantime, companies like Waymo are eating Tesla’s lunch and Google’s Self-Driving Car Project just days ago hired the head of Tesla’s vehicle programs. Waymo has level 4 autonomy, meaning it doesn’t need a driver to be fully operational. Tesla, however, is only at level 2 autonomy, which means a driver needs to be alert and be ready to intervene at the wheel at any time. With Waymo leading Tesla in terms of self-driving tech, I think that the investment outlook for Tesla in the short term is not particularly compelling.

Why The Investment Thesis May Be Faulty

Tesla is obviously not moving toward a near-term catalyst event that could drive a substantial upsurge in net sales. Since the market has also experienced slowing demand for electric-vehicles and new product introductions are years away, I am not convinced I like the catalyst situation for Tesla, particularly in the context of the disappointing Robotaxi event.

With that said, I could definitely be wrong and Tesla’s sales in the electric-vehicle business could soar on recovering EV demand or other reasons. Tesla dominates the EV industry and, in the long run, has sufficiently scaled production and deliveries to justify a high valuation multiple.

My Conclusion

Clearly, as the market’s reaction last week showed us, the Robotaxi event was a dud and did not rise to meet the high expectations that investors had prior to the event. It is quite obvious that it will take years for any of Tesla’s self-driving cars to drive on American roads, and I think that Tesla’s stock rightfully got socked after the Robotaxi event.

Tesla’s 3Q24 deliveries were also lower-than-expected, and I just don’t see a new wave of sales momentum, particularly with Tesla’s vehicle fleet aging and self-driving tech stuck at level 2 autonomy.

Tesla also is very highly valued, based on profits, and the technical chart picture is not particularly compelling: with the stock dropping through the 50-day moving average trendline, I think that the stock has more short-term correction potential, particularly if the company’s 3Q24 earnings disappoint.

Since Tesla has not really been good for major positive surprises as of late, I think the best way to handle Tesla is as a ‘Hold’.

data.title: Baytex Energy: Solid Operational Performance, But Vulnerable To Lower Oil Prices
data.description:
Baytex Energy (NYSE:BTE)(TSX:BTE:CA) has been operationally performing in-line with expectations so far in 2024. It tightened up its full-year production guidance, but left the midpoint of its guidance unchanged.

Baytex is still on track to generate over US$450 million in 2024 free cash flow, but it is vulnerable to lower oil prices since its WTI oil hedges have a floor of US$60. Thus, at current high-$60s WTI strip for 2025, Baytex could generate under US$300 million in free cash flow in a maintenance capex scenario.

I haven't changed my long-term commodity price expectations, but Baytex's estimated value drops by US$0.30 per share compared to June. This is based on the assumption of lower 2025 oil prices based on strip, as well as slightly wider WCS differentials than previously modeled.

My new estimate of Baytex's value is US$4.85 per share at long-term (after 2025) US$75 WTI oil.

This report uses US dollars unless otherwise noted and an exchange rate of CAD$1.38 to US$1.00.

Q2 2024 Results

Baytex reported solid Q2 2024 production results, with production averaging 154,194 BOEPD. This was a 2% increase compared to its Q1 2024 production.

Baytex's oil cut increased slightly from 71% in Q1 2024 to 72% in Q2 2024, as its oil production growth (at +4% quarter-over-quarter) outpaced its total production growth. Heavy oil production was the main driver of the growth, as Baytex's heavy oil production increased by +8% quarter-over-quarter.

Baytex left its full-year guidance generally unchanged, although it narrowed its production guidance range without the midpoint changing.

The WCS differential improved to negative US$13.55 compared to WTI in Q2 2024, helped by the Trans Mountain Pipeline expansion. This was a nearly US$6 improvement from the Q1 2024 WCS differential.

However, more recently the WCS differential has been generally in the US$14 to US$15 range.

This may result in the full-year WCS differential averaging around negative US$15.50, about US$1 wider than Baytex's earlier assumptions from earlier this year (which I thought was reasonable).

Some forecasts had called for the WCS differential to narrow into single digits. That appears too optimistic, but the Trans Mountain Pipeline expansion is still expected to prevent the blowout differentials that have occasionally happened in the past.

Refinery outages and increased competition from Mexican heavy crude imports have been cited as factors causing the WCS differential to remain a bit wider than previously expected.

Updated 2024 Outlook

Baytex tightened its 2024 guidance to a new range of 152,000 BOEPD to 154,000 BOEPD. The guidance midpoint and product composition was unchanged.

The current strip for 2024 includes roughly US$76 WTI oil and US$2.20 NYMEX natural gas. At those commodity prices, Baytex is projected to generate US$2.856 billion in revenues after hedges. This also assumes that the full-year WCS differential is around negative US$15.50.

Estimated Valuation

I previously estimated Baytex's value at US$5.15 per share at long-term US$75 WTI oil and US$3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

I've kept my long-term commodity prices unchanged, but I am using current strip prices until the end of 2025 now. This results in Baytex's estimated value being reduced to approximately US$4.85 per share, despite its operational performance being in-line with expectations.

This reduction is mostly due the impact of WTI strip sitting in the high-$60s for 2025 now. Baytex's oil hedges have a floor of US$60, so it doesn't receive protection from those hedges unless oil prices fall further.

I've also assumed a slightly higher long-term WCS differential (at negative US$14) than what I had modeled (negative US$12 to negative US$13) before. The impact of this is substantially less than the impact of the lower 2025 WTI oil prices, though.

Due to the weaker oil prices, Baytex's free cash flow may end up below US$300 million in 2025 at current strip if its capex budget is relatively similar to 2024.

Conclusion

Baytex has performed in-line with expectations operationally and is on track to meet its 2024 guidance. Baytex's oil hedges consist of WTI collars with a floor of US$60 though, so it is somewhat vulnerable to a moderate fall in oil prices.

At current strip for 2025, the drop in oil prices to the high-$60s could reduce Baytex's free cash flow by close to US$200 million compared to 2024.

I still believe that Baytex offers a decent amount of upside in a long-term US$75 WTI oil environment. If we assume high-$60s WTI oil in 2025 though, that contributes to knocking off US$0.30 per share in value for Baytex.

Thus I now estimate Baytex's value at US$4.85 per share in a long-term (after 2025) US$75 WTI oil environment now. Baytex appears to have a good amount of upside assuming that oil prices rebound after 2025. At sub-US$70 long-term oil, that upside would evaporate unless Baytex's cost structure improves.

data.title: 9%-Yielding Fidus Investment: 133% Dividend Coverage And Low Leverage
data.description:
It’s always nice to have what I would call ‘default’ buys in the market, which I would categorize as high-yielding investments that are reasonably priced, even in as frothy a market as the one we’re in now.

These types of stocks should pay dividends that are well-covered by cash flows, and are not, in other words, “sucker yields”. They should also have a good track record of shareholder returns and have a sound business strategy.

Top of mind names may be ones like Realty Income Corp. (O) or Main Street Capital (MAIN), but in this article, I’m focused on the lesser known, but equally desirable name, Fidus Investment (NASDAQ:FDUS), which is a smaller BDC that doesn’t garner as much attention as bigger names in the space.

I last covered FDUS back in December 2022, highlighting its very strong balance sheet and well-covered regular dividend yield plus appealing special dividends. While the stock has only appreciated by 3.3% since my last piece, income investors know that with BDCs, it’s all about the dividend, and the stock hasn’t disappointed with a 31% total return since my last bullish take from less than 2 years ago.

In this article, I revisit FDUS including recent operating fundamentals, and discuss why it remains an attractive high-yielding bargain for investors, so let’s get started!

Why FDUS?

Fidus Investment is an externally managed BDC that primarily invests in the U.S. middle market, as defined by those companies with annual revenue in the $10 to $150 million range. It was founded in 2007 as a SBIC (small business investment corporation) and became public in 2011.

FDUS’s focus on the lower middle market due to its smaller size enables it to source deals with less competition from big players like Ares Capital (ARCC) and Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC). The LMM is highly fragmented with over 100,000 companies, enabling plenty of greenfield opportunities from which to source deals.

Management is led by a group of veterans, including the CEO, who has been with the company for 16 years and in the industry for 31 years, and no member of the senior leadership team has been with the company for less than 9 years. FDUS also adopts a disciplined deal review process in which it evaluates approximately 600 opportunities per year, of which just 2.5% results in final funding, as illustrated by the review process below.

Importantly, FDUS has delivered a strong track record of value creation through a combination of NAV per share growth and dividends. As shown below, FDUS has delivered nearly 3x its initial NAV per share of $14.46 at the time of IPO in 2011 through a combination of a rising NAV per share and cumulative dividends, which total a combined $42.31.

At present, FDUS carries an investment portfolio with a $1.08 billion fair value, spread across 86 companies. The portfolio is primarily invested in secured debt, which represent 78% of the portfolio, of which 64% is first lien and 15% is second line, with the remainder being subordinated debt (14%) and equity (8%). FDUS’s top 2 sectors are concentrated in information technology and business services, which comprise half the portfolio, as shown below.

Meanwhile, FDUS continues to earn a high weighted average debt yield in the current higher rate environment, with it standing at 14.0% during Q2 2024. The portfolio is also in a healthy shape with a fair value to cost ratio of 101.9% and with investments on non-accrual sitting at just 1.0%, which is below the 2.5% to 3% generally acceptable level for BDCs.

Also encouraging, NAV per share held steady, improving by 0.7% since the start of the year from $19.37 to $19.50. Notably, this sits well above the pre-pandemic NAV per share of $16.81 at the end of 2019. While Adjusted NII per share did decline by $0.02 on a sequential QoQ basis to $0.57, that had to do with the timing of an equity raise during Q2, resulting in more shares.

Looking ahead to Q3 results, I would expect NII per share to increase on a sequential basis, due to deployment of proceeds from the aforementioned equity raise during the second quarter. FDUS traded up to a 6% premium to NAV during Q2, which means that it was able to raise equity in a manner that’s accretive to shareholders.

I would also look for changes to the weighted average yield on investments from the 14.0% level in Q2, considering that the Federal Reserve lowered interest rates by 50 basis points in September. Moreover, I would also monitor the non-accrual rate to see if it changes from the 1.0% level during Q2 (the lower, the better). At the same time, management expects to see a healthy flow of deals in Q3 and for the remainder of the year.

FDUS’s potential forward portfolio growth is supported by a strong balance sheet with a low debt-to-equity ratio of just 0.7x, making it among the least levered BDCs in the industry. Moreover, statutory leverage excluding exempt SBA debentures stands at a low 0.5x, sitting far below the 2.0x regulatory limit for BDCs.

Importantly for income investors, FDUS currently gives an appealing regular dividend yield of 8.8%. The dividend is well-protected by a 133% Adjusted NII-to-Dividend coverage ratio, enabling it to pay a $0.16 special dividend during the third quarter.

Lastly, I continue to see value in FDUS at the current price of $19.54, equating to a Price-to-NAV ratio of 1.0x. This sits in between FDUS's trading range of 0.9 to 1.1x over the past 3 years, as shown below.

FDUS has traded more or less in line with ARCC over the past 3 years, but the valuation gap has widened since the start of September, with ARCC trading at a 1.09x Price-to-Book ratio, as shown below. At the same time, FDUS has higher exposure to first lien loans at 64% of the portfolio compared to ARCC's 50%, and FDUS carries lower leverage compared to ARCC.

Considering FDUS’s strong track record of shareholder returns and its low-leveraged balance sheet, I believe investors could do well by layering into the stock at the current price. With a near 9% regular yield, room for special dividends, and potential for higher NII in the coming quarters as equity proceeds are deployed, investors could potentially see at least market level returns from FDUS.

Risks to the thesis include FDUS’s lower exposure to first-lien debt compared to some other BDCs like Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), which has 99% exposure to first lien. This opens FDUS to potential risks of a macroeconomic downturn, which could result in elevated non-accruals and downward pressure on NII. Moreover, FDUS could see yield compression should interest rates drop faster than expected, and should it see increased competition for deals in the LMM space.

Investor Takeaway

Fidus Investment presents an attractive opportunity for income-focused investors seeking high yield and a well-managed portfolio. This is due to its well-covered 8.8% dividend yield, special dividends, and a disciplined focus on the lower middle market.

Plus, FDUS carries one of the best leverage ratios in the BDC space and has a proven track record of NAV preservation and shareholder returns, which further strengthen its appeal. As such, FDUS’s robust fundamentals and attractive valuation make it an appealing option for long-term investors.

data.title: ROBO: Bet On The Consumers, Not The Innovators
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Robo Global Robotics and Automation Index ETF (NYSEARCA:ROBO) unfortunately seems too diversified to deliver outperformance. Based on the historical performance, the ETF failed to beat the SP 500 over a one, three, and five year time frame. It seems like a simple index fund would do a better job of giving investors exposure to innovative, strong companies that are likely to benefit from automation. Ultimately, I believe the companies that use automation and robotics to improve productivity are the major beneficiaries, not the companies that produce the automation and robotics solutions. Therefore, I rate this ETF as an avoid as it is too diversified, with many of its holdings seemingly fully priced.

The Customer Usually Wins

According to the ETF's official website description,

The ROBO Global Robotics & Automation Index ETF invests in global companies that are driving transformative innovations in robotics, automation, and artificial intelligence, including companies that create technology to enable truly intelligent systems that can sense, process, and act, and companies that apply those technologies to deliver RAAI-enabled products—including robots—to businesses and consumers.

Sounds riveting. However, my opinion is that this industry is brutally competitive, and that the ultimate beneficiary are not these companies but the customer. In other words, the users of this technology are likely to win, not the creators.

When the dot-com frenzy began in the late 90s, many new internet companies had great growth prospects but no clear path to profitability. A land-grab ensued, with companies like NetScape, MySpace, and Pets.com to name a few. Many thought that these companies could grow their business with users, but few actually pondered the true profitability potential of these names. Eventually, when the dot com bubble burst, it became clear that obvious growth did not lead to obvious profits. In the end, the real winners in aggregate weren't the internet companies, but rather us, the users of the internet.

I think we are seeing a similar story here. To me, the real winners in aggregate aren't the companies that create automation technology, but rather the companies that use automation to improve their productivity. It's the consumers that use RAAI-enabled products that win in the aggregate, as they benefit from massively improved productivity and labor cost savings to enhance operational efficiency.

To support my claim that most of these companies don't capture most of the value they create, we can see that some of the ETF's holdings have relatively low profit margins, suggesting that they don't gain that much from offering automation solutions.

Some of these companies in this ETF don't actually make much money, and in some cases still lose money. My take is that some of these companies are unable to capture much of the value they create, as most of the benefit goes to the consumer. These benefits range from automating repetitive workflow, advanced data and market analysis, and enhancing product features. I think most of the R&D and investments these companies make serve to benefit the customer, and not their own bottom lines.

Finally, the ETF seems too diversified to deliver outperformance. With 90 holdings, I think it would be better if they concentrated a little more on promising companies with cheaper valuations and strong growth prospects. The track record agrees with me so far, with this ETF lagging the SP 500 index pretty dramatically since inception.

While the companies in the ROBO ETF may create a lot of value, I think it's the consumers that will capture the majority of that value. The past track record seems to agree with me so far, and the bottom line is that this ETF is both too diversified and unprepared to capture the value that it creates.

Holdings Seem Fairly Priced

According to the ETF's website, the top 5 holdings currently are:

Some of these companies are rather expensive from a valuation multiple perspective. To me, they don't seem to be trading at bargain prices and are already correctly priced, giving investors limited upside from current valuations.

Based on this chart, the valuations for the top 5 holdings except for Nvidia (NVDA) are quite stable. The market seems to have gotten comfortable pricing these companies based on their sales at a stable P/S ratio. While many holdings show earnings, most are already priced at pretty high P/E ratios that already account for their superior growth prospects.

The bottom line is that I believe this ETF has limited upside for investors, with many of its holdings correctly priced based on their valuation multiples. Now that interest rates are no longer near zero, many of these holdings with elevated valuations may be vulnerable to market corrections, and the ETF's risk doesn't seem to be worth the reward.

Unions May Oppose Automation

Recently, the dockworkers strike on October 2024 suggested that some unions and workers reject the idea of automation and robotics in their jobs. According to the union's demands for the dockworkers in the news,

The dockworkers striking up and down the East Coast are, culturally and geographically, a world apart from the Hollywood actors and writers who staged a four-month walkout last year. But their protests share a common core principle: They don’t want bots taking their jobs.

The way this ETF is setup, it is built on the overall success of automating work so that people don't have to do it manually anymore. To me, the unions make no sense as they should be in support of the automation trend to increase their real wages. An increase in real wages can only be supported by an increase in productivity, and the use of robots and automation in their jobs should be a net benefit to their productivity.

In any case, the unions who want to protect their jobs may oppose this automation trend, presenting a headwind for this ETF. Many of the companies in this ETF are innovating robotics and automation solutions that may see pushback from regulators, unions, and other manual workers. I think we have yet to see the full regulatory framework emerge yet, presenting a potential headwind to the companies in this ETF. The bottom line is that the combination of hostile unions and red tape may stall progress and growth prospects for many of the companies in this ETF.

Risks

I don't think this ETF is poised to deliver outperformance. The price refuses to breakout past $60, and the vast majority of the holdings seem fully priced. However, I could be wrong if excitement and new technologies attract more investment attention and cause more money to flow into ETFs like ROBO.

Perhaps it's different this time. The real winners in aggregate could be the companies in the ETF portfolio, in which case my thesis could be invalid. While I believe the main beneficiaries are the consumers, it could be that the innovators that create the technology benefit more than the users. In that case, this ETF could see more upside.

Avoid ROBO

If investors really wanted to benefit from the robotics and automation revolution, a simple SP 500 index fund should suffice. This is because I think the main beneficiary of this trend aren't the creators, but the users. Many of the Fortune 500 companies in the index fund are the true beneficiaries, and will capture most of the value that the ROBO ETF companies create. They can automate their workforce, increase productivity efficiency, and adapt to this new automation revolution to boost their bottom lines.

data.title: Lululemon Has Started A Recovery (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Lululemon (NASDAQ:LULU)(TSX:LLL:CA) appears ready to continue to move across its wide trading range for a gain of at least 20%. The technical factors that indicate this are its long-term relative strength (RSI) and a level of bearish expectation for LULU that has historically occurred just before major advances. Only time will tell whether this move will be the start of a new bull trend.

Lululemon Has Been In A Four-Year Trading Range

The chart below shows the closing price of Lululemon since 2018 and the sideways trend channel that’s contained its price movement for four years. The stock’s made zero gain since the start of COVID in February 2020. The width of the channel is either .50% or 100% depending on which line you’re measuring from.

We believe the $233 July low marked the start of another move across the range. Our minimum price target is $350, which is the middle of the range. At its current price of $287 that would be a further gain of 22%.

Investor Expectations For LULU Are “Too Negative”

You want to avoid a stock when "too many" investors think the price is going higher, and buy it when "too many" think the price is going lower. The indicator shown below helps an investor do this. It measures investor expectations. Green zone readings occur when "too many" investors expect lower prices, while red zone readings occur when "too many" investors expect higher prices.

We've used green arrows to indicate greens on readings in Lulu in the past, and they occurred just before or near intermediate term price rises. The overall decline this year in LULU was accompanied by three clear Green Zone readings; the most recent being during the small price rally of the last few weeks. This level of negativity in the face of a price rise is generally a sign that investors don't think this rally will last. It’s the type of disbelief we like to see, and it usually occurs before a major, intermediate term advance.

Long-Term Relative Strength of LULU Is Positive

This last chart shows the long-term relative strength index for LULU. Most technicians simply use the default parameters for RSI set by the technical website service they use. These are always short term. We calculate our RSI indicator to measure long-term relative strength; the chart below is not a short-term view.

It shows that in July, the long-term RSI reached the same level of being oversold as occurred at the major low during the COVID bear market in 2020. We’ve indicated this with two black arrows. In fact, the low this July went even deeper than the 2020 low. This is a major technical sign that July marked the start of a strong intermediate to long-term advance.

Conclusion

Two powerful technical signals indicate LULU made a major intermediate to long-term price low in July and has started a move back up across its large trading range. A conservative estimate is to move halfway, which would be a price target of $355. We think intermediate-term traders in LULU can safely enter with expectations of a gain of 20% or more. We believe current long-term investors in LULU should hold their position and reassess their situation once this price target is achieved.

data.title: Karooooo: An Overlooked GARP Gem
data.description:
This article emphasizes Karooooo Ltd. (NASDAQ:KARO) as we assess an overlooked asset with growth-at-a-reasonable-price attributes.

For those unaware, Karooooo is a global mobility company that offers software-as-a-service. The company has experienced numerous material events lately, ranging from a second-quarter earnings release to insider stock sales amendments and a volatile stock price, providing us with the necessary latitude to assess its stock.

Without further ado, here's our take on Karooooo's prospects.

Structure And Key Value Drivers

Business Structure

Karooooo offers two major products: Cartrack and Logistical Services. The former is a security management system that tracks vehicles to ensure safety and recoverability, whereas the latter extends beyond Cartrack to provide logistical management software.

The following diagram dispenses the company's business line and geographic revenue mix. Karooooo's Cartrack is focused on South Africa's market, but yields revenue from other regions, such as APAC and EMEA nations.

The company's Cartrack offering delivers much higher profit margins than its logistics and Carzuka (soon to be closed) segments. However, the logistics segment remains at an early stage, whereas Cartrack is a mature business with a substantial presence, meaning a direct comparison might be misleading.

In a nutshell, the company's core business and cash cow are Cartrack. Yet, we believe its logistics segment provides cross-sales synergies while delivering a new growth opportunity, especially through foreign market exposure.

Value Drivers

Karooooo has a few identifiable value drivers. Let's start with Cartrack's value drivers. The business line has shown comprehensive uptake through its subscription model, a sales method that we consider smoother than pure product sales.

Being a South African, I can tell you that vehicle safety is a significant concern, as both light vehicles and trucks have suffered from rising hijackings. The region's safety issues place it in the World Population Review's top ten on index. Despite the negativity of crime, Karooooo's solutions add a positive effect by offering vehicle and fleet owners the necessary tools to protect themselves. Sure, the company profits from its offerings, but it is a win-win situation for it and its customers.

Low unemployment rates, political corruption, and insufficient policing budgets mean demand for vehicle safety tools like Cartrack will likely grow until structural changes occur.

Reverting to Karooooo's broad-based earnings shows that its logistics unit remains a work in progress. However, the segment has shown scintillating growth.

Karooooo's logistics segment revenue increased by 40% year-over-year to R101 million (approximately $5.75 million). The segment's growth rate outpaced Cartrack's by 2.66x, showing its prospects as a catalyst for broad-based expansion.

We think Karooooo's logistics segment has international growth prospects. Cartrack's growth is concentrated, primarily relying on unsafe operating environments, whereas the logistics segment suits any geographic environment. Moreover, the segment benefits from a lean operating model, allowing consumers to scale their operations without investing large sums of capital in internal assets.

Herewith is an overview of Karooooo's logistics segment.

Reviewing Karooooo's Finances

Q2 Earnings Results

Karooooo released its second-quarter earnings report on October 15, revealing a GAAP earnings-per-share beat of one cent and a revenue miss of $853 900. The market reacted strongly, leading to a 15.6% drawdown in its American Depositary Receipts.

Here's a snapshot of Karooooo in pre-market on October 16th.

Despite missing its revenue target, Karooooo's subscription revenue grew by 15% year-over-year to ZAR986 million (approximately $56.16 million), driven by a 17% increase in subscribers. In addition, Karooooo's earnings per share increased by a phenomenal 31% year-over-year, showing signs of margin expansion.

The company maintained its guidance at the midpoint, and despite Karooooo's revenue miss, its real growth remains robust. Therefore, we believe investors have overreacted to its revenue miss.

Balance Sheet and Cash Flows

Karooooo's balance sheet has softened year-over-year. However, it maintains what we consider a solid debtor's days and notable bank deposits, allowing it to maintain its short-term liquidity.

The company's balance sheet is supported by free cash flow growth, which it achieved while realizing outflows from investing activities via an R316 million (about $17.94 million) investment in developing its South African central office.

We think Karooooo remains in good standing, allowing it to reinvest in its business and deliver dividends to its shareholders.

Recent News

Karooooo Refrains From Secondary Offering

Jose Calisto, Karooooo’s CEO, triggered an announced secondary offering in July, allowing the sale of shares worth $75 million in gross proceeds. To our knowledge, Karooooo wouldn't have raised capital through the sale. Instead, it was merely a shareholder seeking divestment.

As you might've gathered, the secondary offer was canceled as the selling shareholder deemed the sale unfavorable. However, this doesn't discount a future sale, as the selling shareholder cited market conditions.

We believe a sale of this magnitude might be executed at a discount, dragging Karooooo's stock price downward. Nevertheless, the CEO's unwillingness to sell might be interpreted as a positive from a company performance standpoint (Karooooo's shares jumped by 7.6% after the announcement).

Wall Street Price Targets

Karooooo received three ratings from Wall Street in the past 90 days. Seeking Alpha's database shows that the stock maintains two buy and one strong buy ratings, with an average price target of $43.58 per share. Although Wall Street ratings can be inaccurate, they often provide positive sentiment-based support as various market participants "buy into" Wall Street's research.

Valuation and Dividend Metrics

A table outlining some of Karooooo's key price multiples is shown below; a discussion follows.

Metric Value
PEG 0.72x
EV/EBITDA 12.26x
Price-to-Cash Flow 15.36x

Source: Seeking Alpha

The stock's price-to-earnings-growth multiple sits below one, suggesting Karooooo's earnings-per-share growth has outpaced its stock price momentum. This observation is particularly important as the stock's non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 25.4x is 9.34% above its five-year average and 5.18% above the sector median.

Furthermore, Karooooo has an EV/EBITDA ratio of 12.26, which is below the sector median of 19.32x. Although the metric is 9.08% above Karooooo's five-year average, the company's rapid growth and margin expansion likely justify an inflated ratio.

Karooooo's price-to-cash flow ratio of 15.36x is below the sector median of 19.65x. Like its EV/EBITDA, Karooooo's P/CF ratio exceeds its five-year average (by 9.08%). As previously shown, the company's cash flows decreased in Q2. However, much of the slowdown can be attributed to an investing outflow.

Finally, Karooooo has a three-year dividend yield on cost of 3.27% and a four-year average dividend yield of 1.84%. We find Karooooo's dividend highly compelling for a growth phase stock.

Risk Factors

Concentration Risk

Karooooo has numerous identifiable risks. Having said that, we consider its primary risk to be operational concentration. The company's excess exposure to South Africa may introduce cyclicality, as much of Karooooo's revenue mix relies on a single economy.

Although it is common for companies to operate in single economies, South Africa is a struggling economy with cyclical attributes. As such, we deem concentration risk a potential overhang.

Some Valuation Metrics Are Questionable

A few price multiples were dispensed in the previous section. We remain bullish about Karooooo's fundamental valuation outlook. Still, some of its price multiples are above their normalized averages (five-year averages). Although the company's growth might justify such valuations, some investors might see things differently, especially as its stock is above its 50-, 100-, and 200-day moving averages.

Closing Thoughts

Karooooo's second-quarter revenue miss spooked the market. However, its headline growth remains exponential, suggesting market participants have overreacted.

Despite likely facing concentration risk and normalized valuation inefficiencies, the company's price-to-earnings ratio conveys Karooooo's fundamental stealth, which derives from noteworthy representation in overlooked SaaS mobility markets.

We think Karooooo is a strong buy.

data.title: Charles Schwab: Solid Investment Setup
data.description:
Shares of brokerage powerhouse Charles Schwab (NYSE:SCHW) were up more than 6% after the company reported better than expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter. The financial services company saw robust top line momentum and strong asset gathering capabilities through the quarter that implies attractive potential for earnings growth. I believe the risk profile has further improved here, and I see upside revaluation potential if the brokerage platform continues to grow its core net new assets. I am rating shares of Charles Schwab a strong buy and see a fair value of $90/share!

Previous ratings

I recommended Charles Schwab aggressively in July after the brokerage firm submitted its second fiscal quarter earnings scorecard in July: This Is A Golden Buying Opportunity. I recommended the financial services company chiefly because of its ability to attract core net new assets to its platform. Additionally, Charles Schwab is highly profitable, which the company’s third-quarter earnings sheet confirmed yet again. I believe shares have revaluation potential, and I am not yet ready to sell.

Better than expected earnings from Charles Schwab in Q3’24

Charles Schwab reported better than expected earnings for its third fiscal quarter, with the financial brokerage earning $0.77 per share, showing a $0.02/share beat. The top line came in at $4.85B, which beat the average prediction by $61M.

Charles Schwab generated $4.8B in net revenues in the third fiscal quarter, showing 5% year-over-year. This growth was driven in part by robust market activity and stock market indices climbing to fresh highs lately. As a major trading platform, Charles Schwab benefits from investors trading in and out of positions in order to take advantage of market volatility.

In the third-quarter, the financial services company also generated $1.41B in earnings, showing a solid 25% year-over-year earnings growth rate. This growth, in part, is due to the brokerage cutting operating expenditures, which has had a positive earnings impact in the third fiscal quarter. As a result, Charles Schwab’s profit margin expanded 8 PP year-over-year to a massive 38% in Q3'24, driven by cost cuts as well as earnings growth that is related to Charles Schwab attracting more client assets to its platform.

Charles Schwab’s client assets grew to $9.92T in Q3'24, showing a massive 27% year-over-year growth. Further, the financial services platform continued to attract a ton of core net new assets, a measure that excludes inflows of new assets related to acquisitions: in Q3’24, Charles Schwab’s core net new assets amounted to $95.3B, showing 109% growth year-over-year.

Zooming out a little bit, we can see that strength in attracting core net new assets was not a singular event: the financial services platform consistently managed to attract assets throughout the year. In the first nine months of FY 2024, Charles Schwab attained $252B in core net new assets, showing 10% year-over-year growth. The brokerage's ability to consistently gather assets from clients -- which are then being put to use on the Charles Schwab platform and which generate fees in return -- is one of the most endearing qualities of an investment in SCHW.

Charles Schwab’s valuation

Charles Schwab, despite a solid 16% gain since my last recommendation in July, is not yet overpriced. The financial services company is expected to earn $3.80 per-share in FY 2025 and estimates have broadly fallen lately, in part due to expectations of weaker net interest margins in a lower-rate world. However, Charles Schwab is undervalued relative to its historical average P/E ratio as well as the valuation ratio of its biggest rival, Interactive Brokers (IBKR).

If Charles Schwab revalues to its 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio of 20.85X, then shares have at least 10% upside potential. The average P/E ratio of 20.85X implies a fair value of $80 per-share. If Charles Schwab would achieve the same valuation level as Interactive Brokers -- which also runs a highly profitable trading platform -- then shares could have a fair value in the neighborhood of $87 per-share.

A revaluation catalyst, in my opinion, lies in the fact that the company is attracting a ton of new assets which will generate fees for Charles Schwab going forward. The brokerage also has a strong history of growing core net new assets consistently, so Charles Schwab is not seeing a whole lot of volatility in its inflows. A higher asset base translates into stronger EPS growth potential going forward, which also points at improving platform profitability.

Risks with Charles Schwab

Charles Schwab is set for long-term asset and earnings growth and looks to be in great financial shape from a profitability perspective. As a trading-focused trading platform, Charles Schwab benefits from volatility which tends to entice customers to engage in more transactions, benefiting the top and bottom line of the financial services company. What would change my mind about Charles Schwab is if the firm were to see falling profitability, lower margins, and a decline in its earnings growth rate.

Final thoughts

Charles Schwab submitted a strong earnings sheet for the third fiscal quarter that once again showed very robust earnings growth and high platform profitability. Yet, shares of the financial brokerage company are still very reasonably valued and currently trade at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of only 18.9X, well below the longer-term average price-to-earnings ratio. With Charles Schwab also yet again demonstrating that it can grow its client assets and attract billions of new core net new assets throughout the quarter, the financial services platform is facing improved earnings growth prospects. In my opinion, shares have an attractive risk profile and not yet overpriced, despite a positive market reaction after the release of Q3'24 earnings. Since Charles Schwab is a general beneficiary of higher market volatility (which translates to higher transaction-related revenues), I believe the brokerage is a solid long-term investment for investors that want to gain exposure to the financial services industry.
